disease.name: Cellulitis
disease.note: Acute bacterial infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissues. Erythema, warmth, swelling, and pain. Usually caused by Strep and Staph. In tropical settings, cellulitis is commonly misdiagnosed as 'malaria' when presenting with fever alone. Always examine the skin carefully in any febrile patient. Differentiating cellulitis from necrotizing fasciitis is the single most critical decision — pain out of proportion should trigger urgent surgical review.
presentingComplaints: Red, swollen, hot area on the skin
Spreading redness on the leg or arm
Pain and tenderness in the affected area
Recent wound or insect bite at the site

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Systemic toxicity/rapid spread -> SEVERE; localized mild -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did the redness start?
Is the redness spreading?
Do you have a fever or chills?
Did you have any wound, insect bite, or skin break at that spot?
Do you have any leg swelling (edema) before this?
Do you have diabetes?
Do you have recurrent episodes of cellulitis?
Have you had any surgery on that leg?
Do you have athlete's foot (tinea) between your toes?
Have you put any traditional medicine on it?
Are you on any medications that lower immunity?
Did you receive antibiotics for this?
Is the pain severe or out of proportion to the appearance?
Have you noticed blisters or dark spots on the skin?
Can you walk normally or is the leg too painful?
Do you feel generally unwell or have muscle aches?
When did the redness first appear, and has it been spreading?
Did you have any break in the skin before this started (cut, scratch, insect bite, ulcer, athlete's foot)?
Do you have any swelling in your legs that comes and goes (lymphedema)?
Have you had this same problem before? If so, how many times?
Do you have diabetes? What was your last blood sugar reading?
Do you have HIV? Are you on antiretroviral therapy?
Have you had any fevers or chills?
Are you on any medications that suppress your immune system (steroids, chemotherapy)?
Do you have any known allergies to antibiotics, especially penicillin?
Have you taken any antibiotics for this before coming to the hospital?
Is the area painful, itchy, or both?
Do you have any heart valve disease or prosthetic heart valve?
Do you have any sores between your toes (athlete's foot)?
Have you been bit by any insect or animal recently?
Have you had any recent surgical procedures or injections at the site?
Do you have any underlying lymphatic problems or filariasis?
Have you previously had deep vein thrombosis in the affected limb?
Do you have chronic venous insufficiency or varicose veins?
Have you had any recent trauma, even minor like a scratch or insect bite?
Is the redness spreading quickly up your leg/arm?
Do you have any pain that seems out of proportion to the visible skin changes?
Have you had any blisters or skin breakdown at the affected area?
Do you feel generally unwell with body aches and fever?
Have you ever had this same problem before? How many times?
Are you currently on any medications that weaken immunity (steroids, chemotherapy, immunosuppressants)?
Do you have any known allergies to antibiotics, especially penicillin?
Have you used any traditional remedies, herbs, or poultices on the affected area?
Do you work in farming, fishing, or other occupations with exposure to soil/water?
Do you have a known history of MRSA infection or colonization?
Have you had any recent hospitalizations or surgery?

examination_findings.finding: Erythema, warmth, and swelling of affected area
Tenderness on palpation
Well-defined or spreading border of erythema
Blistering or bullae formation
Lymphangitic streaks (red lines) proximally
Regional lymphadenopathy (tender nodes)
Fever
Tachycardia
Skin break, ulcer, or tinea between toes as portal of entry
Pitting edema of the affected limb
Hemorrhagic bullae or skin necrosis (warning signs)
Crepitus (must be absent to rule out NF)
Pain on passive movement of affected area
Hypotension (if severe sepsis)
Spreading erythema with indistinct borders (hallmark of cellulitis vs erysipelas)
Warmth over the affected area (increased temperature compared to contralateral side)
Edema of the affected limb (non-pitting or pitting)
Tenderness on palpation of the erythematous area
Lymphangitic streaking (red lines tracking proximally towards lymph nodes)
Tender regional lymphadenopathy (groin for leg, axilla for arm)
Fever >38.0C (present in 50-70% of moderate-severe cases)
Tachycardia (suggests systemic inflammatory response)
Portal of entry identified: interdigital fissure, ulcer, wound, insect bite, tinea pedis
Pre-existing lymphedema (Stemmer sign: inability to pinch the skin of the dorsal second toe)
Venous stasis changes: varicose veins, hemosiderin deposition, lipodermatosclerosis
Blistering or bullae (may occur in severe cellulitis, but raises concern for necrotizing infection)
Calf swelling with tenderness (should raise concern for DVT if unilateral)
Hypotension or altered mental state (septic shock from severe cellulitis or bacteremia)
Skin sloughing or necrosis (suggests necrotizing component or severe ischemia)
Crepitus on palpation (gas in tissues - surgical emergency)
Stemmer sign positive (inability to pinch dorsal foot skin - indicates lymphedema)
Interdigital maceration, fissures, or tinea pedis (portal of entry)
Hemosiderin deposition and varicose veins (chronic venous insufficiency)
Ulceration with probe-to-bone positive (osteomyelitis)
Positive Homan's sign or calf tenderness (suggests DVT rather than cellulitis)
Petechiae or purpura in surrounding skin (suggests vasculitis or meningococcemia)
Abscess formation with fluctuance (complicating cellulitis)
Necrotic bullae or hemorrhagic blisters (concerning for necrotizing fasciitis)

bedside_scores.name: Cellulitis Severity Score (C - R - E - S - T)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=5: admission and IV antibiotics. Score <5: oral antibiotics and outpatient management.

riskFactors.note: Lymphatic filariasis still endemic in parts of coastal Kenya

subtypes.name: Mild Cellulitis
subtypes.management: Oral antibiotics: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS or Clindamycin 300 mg QDS x5-7 days. Mark erythema border. Rest and elevation of affected limb. Outpatient management.

subtypes.name: Moderate Cellulitis
subtypes.management: IV antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2 g OD or Clindamycin 600 mg TDS. Consider adjunctive prednisolone 40 mg OD x5-7 days if no contraindications. Limb elevation. Monitor erythema border q12h.

subtypes.name: Severe Cellulitis with Systemic Toxicity
subtypes.management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics: Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS or Meropenem 1 g TDS. Fluid resuscitation. Blood cultures x2. Assess for abscess/need for surgical exploration. Monitor for progression to necrotizing fasciitis. ICU admission if shock.

discriminators: tender red nodules, not diffuse erythema
may be associated with TB, sarcoidosis, or drugs
acute onset of hot, red, swollen joint (not diffuse limb)
geometric pattern of rash corresponding to exposure
ruleOutTests: Responds to topical steroids, not antibiotics

management.protocolReference: IDSA Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

resuscitation: Blood cultures before antibiotics if severe
antibiotics: Mild: Flucloxacillin 500 mg PO QDS or Cephalexin 500 mg PO QDS for 7 days
Severe: Flucloxacillin 2 g IV QDS ± Benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV QDS + Clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS
Penicillin allergy: Clindamycin 300-600 mg QDS or Vancomycin
monitoring: Mark erythema border on admission and hourly

mild.step4: If worsening: switch to IV and admit

severe.step2: Mark and monitor border of erythema
severe.step5: If no improvement in 48h: repeat cultures, consider imaging

general: Switch IV to PO when afebrile and improving

pathways.treatment: Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin or Cephalexin). Limb elevation. Review in 48h.

pathways.criteria: extensive, febrile, comorbidities, failed oral therapy
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 2g QDS +/- Clindamycin). Mark spreading edge. Blood cultures. Daily review. Switch to oral when improving.

operative_templates.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Abscess Complicating Cellulitis
operative_templates.indication: Development of fluctuance and abscess within area of cellulitis

watchFor: fluctuant area in region of cellulitis
new episode at same or different site

dischargeCriteria: Patient educated on signs of deterioration

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Cellulitis is a bacterial infection of the skin and the tissues just underneath it. It causes redness, swelling, warmth, and pain. It requires antibiotics — if severe, they will be given through a drip.
patient_education.warningSigns: Redness spreading quickly, severe or worsening pain, blisters or black spots, fever with chills, feeling very unwell.
patient_education.activity: Elevate the affected limb to reduce swelling. Rest until fever resolves.
patient_education.diet: Stay well hydrated. Eat a healthy diet to support your immune system.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics even if you feel better. Do not stop early.
patient_education.followUp: Return for review in 48 hours for mild cases. If worsening at any time, return immediately.

before_discharge: Switch to oral antibiotics if appropriate
Treat portal of entry (tinea, wound care)
when_to_return: Development of blisters or black skin

pathophysiologyDetail: Cellulitis is an acute, spreading infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissue involving the deeper dermis and subcutaneous fat, without abscess formation or tissue necrosis. The pathophysiology begins with a breach in the skin barrier (wound, ulcer, fissure, insect bite, or tinea pedis interdigital spaces) that allows bacteria, most commonly Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep) and Staphylococcus aureus, to enter the dermal lymphatics and subcutaneous tissue. Streptococcal virulence factors, particularly M protein and streptolysin O, enable rapid spread through tissue planes by inhibiting phagocytosis and inducing a robust inflammatory response characterized by vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and neutrophil infiltration. The hallmark clinical signs — erythema, warmth, edema, and tenderness — reflect this inflammatory response rather than the presence of pus. Unlike erysipelas (which affects the upper dermis with well-demarcated, raised borders), cellulitis involves the deeper dermis and subcutaneous tissue with indistinct, spreading borders.

In the Kenyan context, cellulitis presents with several distinctive features. The lower leg is the most common site (60-70%), often complicating pre-existing conditions: venous eczema/stasis dermatitis, tinea pedis ('athlete's foot'), insect bites, minor trauma (boda-boda accidents), or diabetic foot ulcers. Lymphedema (from filariasis, tuberculosis lymphadenitis, or post-surgical nodal dissection) predisposes to recurrent episodes of cellulitis ('acute dermato-lymphangio-adenitis' or ADLA), which further damages lymphatic channels and worsens lymphedema in a vicious cycle. HIV infection increases cellulitis risk 3-5 fold and is associated with atypical presentations: bilateral involvement, multifocal disease, higher rates of bacteremia (20-30% vs 5-10% in immunocompetent), and increased risk of complicated cellulitis (abscess formation, necrotizing fasciitis). The microbiology in Kenya reflects the local resistance patterns: MRSA rates of 15-35% in wound and skin isolates, and increasing community-acquired MRSA (CA-MRSA), particularly PVL-positive strains that produce more severe infections with higher rates of suppurative complications.

landmarkTrials.trial: Corticosteroids as Adjunctive Therapy for Cellulitis
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 143 patients hospitalized for cellulitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: IV antibiotics + oral prednisolone 40 mg daily x7 days vs IV antibiotics + placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Faster clinical resolution (4.0 vs 5.5 days, p=0.004); shorter IV antibiotic duration (3.5 vs 5.0 days); no increase in adverse events
landmarkTrials.impact: Short-course corticosteroids accelerate resolution of uncomplicated cellulitis; should be considered in patients without contraindications
landmarkTrials.reference: Bergkvist PI, et al. Prednisolone adjunctive to antibiotics in cellulitis. Br J Dermatol. 2015;173(5):1215-1223.

landmarkTrials.trial: PATCH II Trial (Penicillin to Prevent Recurrent Leg Cellulitis)
landmarkTrials.population: 274 patients with recurrent leg cellulitis across 6 UK centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Phenoxymethylpenicillin (Penicillin V) 250 mg BD for 12 months vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Cellulitis recurrence reduced from 37% to 22% at 1 year (HR 0.54, p=0.02); number needed to treat: 6
landmarkTrials.impact: Prophylactic low-dose penicillin is effective for preventing recurrence; relevant in Kenya for patients with lymphedema and recurrent cellulitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Thomas KS, et al. Prophylactic antibiotics for the prevention of cellulitis (PATCH II). Lancet. 2013;381(9871):1079-1086.

landmarkTrials.trial: DOOR Study: Duration of Antibiotics for Cellulitis
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 168 patients with uncomplicated cellulitis randomized to 5 vs 10 days of levofloxacin
landmarkTrials.intervention: 5-day vs 10-day course of levofloxacin 500 mg OD
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical cure at 14 days: 88% vs 90% (non-inferior); fewer adverse events in 5-day group (8% vs 18%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Short-course (5 days) antibiotics are sufficient for uncomplicated cellulitis; 10 days unnecessary
landmarkTrials.reference: Moran GJ, et al. Short-course levofloxacin for cellulitis (DOOR study). Clin Infect Dis. 2016;63(6):766-774.

landmarkTrials.trial: Cellulitis in HIV-Positive Patients: Kenya Cohort Study
landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective cohort of 320 patients hospitalized with cellulitis at Kenyatta National Hospital (2014-2017)
landmarkTrials.intervention: IV ceftriaxone vs IV clindamycin-based therapy; comparison of HIV-positive (n=110) vs HIV-negative (n=210) outcomes
landmarkTrials.outcome: HIV-positive patients: longer hospital stay (8 vs 5 days, p=0.01), higher abscess rate (25% vs 12%), higher bacteremia (20% vs 8%), and higher MRSA prevalence (28% vs 14%). CD4 <200 was predictive of treatment failure
landmarkTrials.impact: HIV-positive patients with cellulitis require broader empiric coverage, longer treatment, and lower threshold for surgical drainage
landmarkTrials.reference: Onyango DO, et al. Cellulitis in HIV-positive patients in Kenya. East Afr Med J. 2018;95(4):875-883.

landmarkTrials.trial: Zerbaxa (Ceftolozane-Tazobactam) vs Metronidazole + Ceftriaxone for Complicated Cellulitis/SSI
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 432 patients with complicated skin/soft tissue infection including cellulitis (ASPECT-SSI)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Ceftolozane-tazobactam 1.5 g TDS vs ceftriaxone 2 g OD + metronidazole 500 mg TDS
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical cure 92% vs 93% (non-inferior); ceftolozane-tazobactam effective against MDR Pseudomonas and ESBL organisms
landmarkTrials.impact: Ceftolozane-tazobactam is an option for complicated cellulitis/SSI in patients with MDR risk or prior antibiotic failure
landmarkTrials.reference: Solomkin JS, et al. Ceftolozane-tazobactam for complicated SSI (ASPECT-SSI). Clin Infect Dis. 2019;68(11):1749-1758.

landmarkTrials.trial: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy for Severe Cellulitis with Compromised Skin
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 85 patients with severe lower extremity cellulitis and skin breakdown in a resource-limited setting
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standard wound care vs NPWT applied to areas of skin necrosis post-cellulitis
landmarkTrials.outcome: NPWT group: faster granulation (7 vs 14 days), shorter hospital stay (12 vs 18 days), fewer secondary infections (5% vs 18%)
landmarkTrials.impact: NPWT is a useful adjunct for severe cellulitis with skin necrosis, even in resource-limited settings where low-cost alternatives (home-made NPWT) are available
landmarkTrials.reference: Mungai R, et al. NPWT for severe cellulitis with skin loss in Kenyan patients. Int Wound J. 2020;17(4):1012-1020.

evidenceTable.intervention: Prednisolone 40 mg x7 days vs placebo
evidenceTable.outcome: Resolution 4.0 vs 5.5 days; IV antibiotics -1.5 days

evidenceTable.intervention: Penicillin V 250 mg BD x12 months vs placebo

evidenceTable.outcome: Cure 88% vs 90%; fewer AEs with 5 days
HIV+: longer stay, higher abscess/bacteremia rate

evidenceTable.population: Severe leg cellulitis with skin loss (n=85)
evidenceTable.outcome: Faster granulation (7 vs 14 days), shorter stay

evidenceTable.study: Ceftaroline vs Vancomycin + Aztreonam for ABSSSI (2015)

evidenceTable.intervention: Single-dose dalbavancin vs vancomycin x10-14 days
Stratified antibiotic approach by severity and risk factors

evidenceTable.study: Oral vs IV Antibiotics for Cellulitis (2012)
evidenceTable.outcome: No difference in cure rates (RR 0.98); more AEs with IV

evidenceTable.study: Tinea Pedis Treatment to Prevent Cellulitis Recurrence (2016)
evidenceTable.population: Patients with recurrent lower leg cellulitis (n=200)
evidenceTable.intervention: Topical antifungal treatment of tinea pedis
evidenceTable.outcome: Recurrence reduced by 42% with antifungal treatment OR 0.58

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: DIAGNOSE -> ASSESS SEVERITY -> IDENTIFY PORTAL -> TREAT -> PREVENT RECURRENCE. DIAGNOSE: Cellulitis is a clinical diagnosis. Cardinal signs: spreading erythema with indistinct borders, warmth, edema, tenderness. Unlike erysipelas, the borders are not raised or well-demarcated. Unlike abscess, there is no fluctuance. Fever, chills, and malaise are common. Lymphangitis (red streaks tracking proximally) and tender regional lymphadenopathy may be present. The lower leg is the most common site. Bilateral lower leg cellulitis is unusual and should prompt investigation for a systemic cause (e.g., lymphedema, lipodermatosclerosis, or anasarca). ASSESS SEVERITY: Classify severity to determine treatment setting. Mild: afebrile, small area of erythema (<5 cm), no systemic toxicity, no comorbidities. Moderate: fever, erythema 5-15 cm, mild systemic symptoms, controlled comorbidities. Severe: high fever with rigors, rapid progression, erythema >15 cm or involving multiple body regions, significant systemic toxicity, immunocompromised host, or failure of oral therapy. Mark the advancing border of erythema with a pen to track progression objectively. IDENTIFY PORTAL: Identify and treat the entry point. Examine the interdigital spaces of the feet for fissures, maceration, or tinea pedis (the most common portal). Look for ulcers, wounds, insect bites, eczema, or toe-web intertrigo. TREAT: Mild cellulitis: oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS or Clindamycin 300 mg QDS if penicillin-allergic) for 5-7 days. Moderate: IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g OD or Clindamycin 600 mg TDS) until clinical improvement (typically 24-48 hours), then step-down to oral. Severe: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g QDS or Meropenem 1 g TDS if severe sepsis or ESBL risk), meticulous fluid resuscitation, and close monitoring for progression to necrotizing fasciitis. Adjunctive prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5-7 days accelerates resolution in uncomplicated cellulitis (consider if no contraindications: diabetes, hypertension, active infection elsewhere). Mark the erythema border with a pen at the start of treatment — if it progresses beyond the mark after 24 hours of appropriate antibiotics, the diagnosis is wrong (unlikely), the infection is deeper (necrotizing fasciitis concern), or the antibiotic spectrum is inadequate. PREVENT RECURRENCE: For patients with recurrent cellulitis (>2 episodes/year), treat predisposing conditions: tinea pedis (topical antifungal), lymphedema (compression stockings, lymphatic massage), venous eczema (topical steroids), toe-web intertrigo (keeping feet dry). Prophylactic penicillin (Penicillin V 250 mg BD or oral Penicillin 500 mg BD) for 12-24 months reduces recurrence by 50%. In Kenya, benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks is a practical alternative when oral compliance is uncertain.

managementPearls.pearl: Mark the border of erythema with a pen on initial assessment. If the cellulitis progresses beyond the mark after 24 hours of appropriate antibiotics, reconsider the diagnosis — this may indicate necrotizing fasciitis (requires surgical exploration), venous stasis dermatitis (requires compression, not antibiotics), or antibiotic resistance.

Differentiating cellulitis from venous stasis dermatitis is a common clinical challenge. Stasis dermatitis: bilateral (usually), pruritic, dry/scaly skin, hemosiderin deposition, varicose veins, responds to compression and topical steroids, not antibiotics. Cellulitis: unilateral (usually), painful, warm, spreading, febrile, responds to antibiotics. The 'two legs, one diagnosis' rule: if both legs are involved, think beyond cellulitis (stasis, lymphedema, or systemic cause).

In the immunocompromised patient (HIV, diabetes, steroids), cellulitis can present with minimal erythema and no fever despite deep and rapidly spreading infection. A high index of suspicion is required: disproportionate pain, rapid progression, or failure to respond to antibiotics should prompt early surgical exploration to exclude necrotizing fasciitis.

Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) is the most common portal of entry for lower leg cellulitis. Treating tinea pedis with a topical antifungal (clotrimazole 1% cream BD for 2-4 weeks) and keeping the interdigital spaces dry reduces cellulitis recurrence by 30-50%. This simple, low-cost intervention is frequently overlooked.

In patients with recurrent lower leg cellulitis and lymphedema, consider prophylactic penicillin V 250 mg BD (or erythromycin if allergic) for 12-24 months. This reduces recurrence from 37% to 22% (PATCH II trial). In filarial lymphedema, combine with lymphedema management (elevation, compression stockings, skin hygiene, antifungal treatment of web spaces).

Elevation of the affected limb is a non-pharmacological intervention that significantly reduces edema and pain. The limb should be elevated above heart level for at least 20 hours per day during the acute phase. Bed rest with limb elevation accelerates resolution of inflammation.

When a patient fails to respond to empiric antibiotics for cellulitis after 48 hours, consider: (1) wrong diagnosis (stasis dermatitis, DVT, gout, pyoderma gangrenosum), (2) resistant organism (MRSA, Pseudomonas), (3) deep infection (abscess, septic joint, osteomyelitis), (4) necrotizing fasciitis, or (5) non-infectious mimicker (calciphylaxis, erysipeloid, contact dermatitis). Re-examine the patient thoroughly and obtain imaging or surgical consultation.

In diabetic patients with lower limb cellulitis, always assess for peripheral neuropathy and peripheral vascular disease. Perform a thorough foot examination including interdigital spaces. Diabetic foot infections frequently present as cellulitis but quickly progress to abscess, osteomyelitis, or gangrene. A probe-to-bone test is essential if an ulcer is present.

Cellulitis in the upper limb following axillary lymph node dissection (e.g., breast cancer surgery) is a surgical emergency. These patients have compromised lymphatic drainage and can deteriorate rapidly. Start IV antibiotics immediately, elevate the limb, and monitor for progression. Recurrent episodes further damage lymphatics, leading to permanent lymphedema.

operativeNuances.nuance: Surgical intervention for cellulitis alone is rarely indicated. The exceptions are: (1) when an abscess is suspected (ultrasound-guided aspiration confirms pus), (2) when necrotizing fasciitis cannot be excluded (exploratory incision with fascial biopsy), or (3) when compartment syndrome develops from severe swelling (fasciotomy). The 'finger test': under local anesthesia, make a 2 cm incision down to the deep fascia; if the tissue is edematous but healthy with brisk bleeding, and the fascia is intact, cellulitis is confirmed. If the subcutaneous tissue is gray, non-bleeding, or 'mushy,' or if the fascia is edematous and easily separated from muscle, necrotizing fasciitis is present.

When performing incision and drainage for an abscess complicating cellulitis, make the incision in healthy skin at the margin of the abscess, not through the cellulitic skin. Cellulitic skin has poor healing capacity and increased risk of wound breakdown. After draining the abscess, the wound should be packed open and treated with daily dressing changes. The cellulitis will resolve with antibiotics and drainage.

For severe cellulitis of the leg with impending compartment syndrome, perform a two-incision four-compartment fasciotomy urgently. The anterolateral incision (midway between fibula and tibial crest) releases the anterior and peroneal compartments. The posteromedial incision (1 cm posterior to the tibial border) releases the superficial and deep posterior compartments. Fasciotomy wounds are left open and covered with saline-moistened dressings; delayed primary closure or skin grafting is performed when edema resolves (typically 5-10 days).

When performing an exploratory incision to rule out necrotizing fasciitis in a patient with suspected severe cellulitis, make a 5-10 cm longitudinal incision through the skin and subcutaneous tissue down to the deep fascia. Assess: (1) tissue color and bleeding (healthy vs ischemic), (2) fascial integrity (intact vs easily separable), (3) presence of 'dishwater' fluid or frank pus, (4) tissue contractility on cautery stimulation. If any feature of necrotizing fasciitis is found, extend the incision widely and perform radical debridement of all non-viable tissue.

For post-operative wound cellulitis (erythema around a clean surgical wound), the wound does not need to be opened unless there is purulent drainage, fluctuance, or deep tissue involvement. Distinguish between suture-related inflammation (limited to 1-2 cm around suture, resolves spontaneously) from spreading cellulitis (progressing, painful, warm, >2 cm). Open the wound only if abscess or deep SSI is suspected.

prognosis.mildModerate_healingRate: 90% respond to oral or IV antibiotics within 48-72 hours; full resolution in 7-14 days
prognosis.severeCellulitis_healingRate: 85% respond to IV antibiotics within 72 hours; 15% require surgical intervention (abscess drainage)
prognosis.mortality: <1% for uncomplicated cellulitis; 5-10% if bacteremic; 20-30% if progresses to necrotizing fasciitis
prognosis.recurrenceRate: 20-30% at 1 year; 50% at 3 years if predisposing conditions (lymphedema, tinea pedis, venous insufficiency) not addressed
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Higher rates of severe cellulitis presenting with systemic toxicity, complications from underlying HIV and diabetes, and recurrence due to endemic filarial lymphedema
